name: Acorn BBC BASIC
description: BBC BASIC is the BASIC interpreter built into the BBC Micro, written by Sophie Wilson at Acorn Computers. It is a 16 kB sideways language ROM combining a line editor, a keyword tokeniser and de-tokeniser, a statement interpreter, an expression evaluator with full operator precedence, integer and 40-bit floating-point arithmetic, string handling, and variable storage. BBC BASIC is renowned for its structured-programming features — named procedures and functions (PROC/FN) with local variables, REPEAT/UNTIL loops, and IF/THEN/ELSE — and for its built-in 6502 assembler, which lets machine code be written inline and assembled directly from a BASIC program. Version II is the most widely used BBC Micro release.

analyses.label: Control flow on five stacks (BASIC II)
analyses.note: How BBC BASIC II spreads control flow across five stacks — the 6502 hardware stack, the BASIC value stack, and the FOR, REPEAT and GOSUB arrays in page 5 — and why a PROC/FN call saves the first two but not the loop stacks.

analyses.label: The 5-byte float format and the precision of BASIC's constants (BASIC II)
analyses.note: A reading of BBC BASIC II's packed 5-byte floating-point format and its constant pool: how the 32-bit significand encodes values such as e, pi/180 and ln 2, the inconsistent last-bit rounding across them, and the two-part Cody–Waite pi/2.

analyses.label: Tokenising and de-tokenising: the keyword table, the crunch, and LIST (BASIC II)
analyses.note: An address-cited account of how BBC BASIC II turns source text into tokens and back: the 126-entry keyword/token/flag table, the flag bits, the first-match crunch and its suppression contexts, the three-byte line-number encoding, the line storage layout, and what LIST preserves.

analyses.label: Data files: channels, raw bytes, and the backwards PRINT# (BASIC II)
analyses.note: How BBC BASIC II handles data files: the channel-based I/O behind OPENIN/OPENOUT/OPENUP, CLOSE, BGET#/BPUT#, PRINT#/INPUT# and PTR#/EXT#/EOF#, each a wrapper over a MOS call, and the PRINT#/INPUT# record format that stores a type tag then the value's bytes in reverse.

analyses.label: Indirection lvalues as PROC/FN parameters (BASIC II)
analyses.note: A BBC BASIC II capability: a DEF FN/DEF PROC formal parameter may be any assignable location — a byte (?), word (!) or string ($) indirection onto memory — because the parameter binder reuses parse_lvalue, so binding such a formal is a scoped assignment to a memory cell.

analyses.label: Parsers and evaluators: who reads text, and how the language reaches them (BASIC II)
analyses.note: A road-map to BBC BASIC II's text-reading machinery: the tokeniser, the statement dispatcher, the recursive-descent expression evaluator, the factor-level readers, and the lvalue parser. Explains why VAL("&FF") is 0 but EVAL("&FF") is 255 — the decimal and hex readers are distinct routines.

analyses.label: Program lifecycle: what NEW, OLD, CLEAR, RUN and CHAIN inherit and leave behind (BASIC II)
analyses.note: What state survives, and what is wiped, across NEW, OLD, CLEAR, RUN and CHAIN. All five funnel through clear_vars_heap_stack, which empties the dynamic-variable world but preserves the resident integers @% and A%–Z% at &0400–&046B — the channel for passing data into a CHAINed program.

analyses.label: The inline 6502 assembler (BASIC II)
analyses.note: How BBC BASIC II's [ ... ] inline 6502 assembler works: a thin front end over the interpreter's expression evaluator and variable table. Covers labels as BASIC variables, P% and O%, the OPT flag bits, the packed mnemonic table, zero-page/absolute auto-sizing, the EQU directives, and the forward-reference mechanism behind two-pass assembly.

references.label: tobylobster's BBC Micro MOS disassembly
references.note: tobylobster's fully annotated disassembly of the BBC Micro MOS — a reference for the OSBYTE / OSWORD / vector-call semantics and zero-page / workspace names BBC BASIC relies on.

references.label: The Advanced BASIC ROM User Guide (Colin Pharo)
references.note: A detailed reference to the internals of the BBC BASIC ROM: zero-page usage, the keyword table, the interpreter loop, variable storage, and the floating-point routines.

references.label: BBC Microcomputer System User Guide (John Coll)
references.note: The official BBC Micro user guide. Much of it documents the BBC BASIC language: statements, functions, operators, and the assembler.

references.label: J.G. Harston's BBC BASIC source reconstruction
references.note: A multi-target reconstruction of the BBC BASIC source that reassembles (with the BBC BASIC assembler) to byte-identical ROMs, with zero-page maps and entry/exit documentation. The primary external source of BBC BASIC II labels.

references.label: tobylobster's BBC BASIC tokeniser/detokeniser
references.note: A Python BBC BASIC tokeniser and detokeniser — a clean reference for the keyword/token table and the tokenising rules.

references.label: hoglet's BBC BASIC 4r32 disassembly
references.note: A byte-identical disassembly of BBC BASIC 4r32. A later, reorganised cousin of BASIC II, useful for cross-referencing structure and label names.

references.label: BBC BASIC II in The BBC Micro ROM Library
references.note: The exact ROM image catalogued by tobylobster's ROM library, identified by MD5.
